meta.title: Mercedes SLS AMG Black Series Engine Replacement Cost UK
meta.description: Every Mercedes‑Benz SLS AMG Black Series engine — M159 6.2 V8. C197 generation, real UK costs and reliability. Get free quotes from 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists today.

Mercedes‑Benz SLS AMG Black Series Engine Codes

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes‑Benz SLS AMG Black Series engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned M159 engine typically costs £10,000–£15,000 supply only, with rebuilt units ranging from £12,000–£18,000. Fitting adds £1,200–£1,800. Main dealer new engines can exceed £35,000. Engine Finders reports savings of up to £3,500 versus dealer on common M159 repairs. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes‑Benz SLS AMG Black Series engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M159 is rated ★★★★☆ — it's one of the greatest naturally aspirated V8s ever built. However, it has known issues: oil starvation under high-G cornering (addressed by AMG Technical Bulletin TSB-AMG-11-03), valvetrain wear (largely resolved on the Black Series with DLC-coated tappets but camshaft adjusters can still fail), and DCT transmission issues under aggressive use. Proper maintenance is essential.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes‑Benz SLS AMG Black Series?

acceptedAnswer.text: M159 oil starvation during aggressive cornering (requires upgraded dry-sump scavenge pump), valvetrain wear (camshaft adjuster failure and tappet wear), DCT transmission issues under high-rpm shifting, head bolt issues on pre-April 2011 engines, and a driver's airbag recall. The oil starvation issue is the most critical — it can cause catastrophic bearing failure.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes‑Benz SLS AMG Black Series engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value. Against a well‑kept SLS AMG Black Series worth £250,000–£400,000, a £10,000–£18,000 recon is a manageable 2.5–7% of the vehicle's value — absolutely worth it. Given the Black Series's status as one of the greatest modern Mercedes-AMG supercars and its strong investment-grade value, replacement is almost always viable.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes‑Benz SLS AMG Black Series engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The Black Series uses the M159.980 6.2‑litre naturally aspirated V8. Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the M159's oil starvation issue, or does it need a new engine?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — if caught early. Upgrading the dry-sump scavenge pump with reinforced scavenge circuits and revised windage trays per AMG Technical Bulletin TSB-AMG-11-03 costs £2,500–£5,000. This is essential preventative maintenance for any Black Series that sees track use. If oil starvation has already caused bearing failure, a full engine replacement is usually required — far more expensive than the preventative upgrade.

rows.bestFor: Insurance jobs, warranty claims; dealer costs can exceed £35,000

replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an SLS AMG Black Series engine typically takes 16–20 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer). Engine Finders reports savings of up to £3,500 versus dealer on common M159 oil pump and timing guide repairs. Rebuilt M159 units typically come with a 12-month unlimited-mileage warranty.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Mercedes-Benz SLS AMG Black Series — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Upgrade dry-sump scavenge pump per TSB-AMG-11-03; essential for track use
Replace with upgraded DLC-coated tappets if not already fitted
Replace adjusters; catch it before timing issues
Replace with updated head bolts; affects early M156/M159 engines
Regular fluid changes prevent many issues
Check recall status — dealer will replace free of charge

buyingChecks.title: Buying This SLS AMG Black Series? Check These First
buyIf: * Full Mercedes-AMG service history with documented oil changes
* Evidence of dry-sump scavenge pump upgrade (TSB-AMG-11-03 completed)
* DLC-coated valve tappets (standard on Black Series from factory)
* No history of track use without oiling system upgrades
avoidIf: * Oil pressure warnings under cornering with no oiling system history
* Loud tapping on cold start — potential valvetrain or camshaft adjuster issues
* Harsh shifting or hesitation — DCT issues
* Any evidence of bearing failure or oil starvation
* Poor service history — the M159 is unforgiving of neglect

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The SLS AMG Black Series is one of the greatest naturally aspirated V8 supercars ever built — 631 bhp, an 8,000 rpm redline, and a soundtrack to die for. The M159 is a masterpiece of engineering, but it has known weaknesses: oil starvation under high-G cornering (addressed by AMG TSB-AMG-11-03), valvetrain wear (largely resolved on the Black Series with DLC-coated tappets but camshaft adjusters can still fail), and DCT transmission issues under aggressive use. Buy on documented service history and proof of oiling system upgrades. If you're replacing, a reconditioned unit with upgraded oil pump gears and revised crankcase ventilation is essential.

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes‑Benz SLS AMG Black Series (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Oil Pressure — Any history of oil pressure warnings? Oil starvation is the most critical issue
* Cold Start — Listen for tapping or clattering on startup (valvetrain or camshaft adjuster warning)
* Service History — Including which engine era (M159.980 is the only Black Series engine — verify this before purchase) and evidence of TSB-AMG-11-03 work
* Track Use — Has the car been tracked? If so, has the oiling system been upgraded?
* Transmission — Any harsh shifting or hesitation? DCT issues are common under aggressive use
* Head Bolt Recall — For earlier engines, check if head bolts have been updated
* Airbag Recall — Has the driver's airbag recall been completed?
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved timing, valvetrain, or oil pressure issues?
